title: wallet restore uses hidden input for 24-word mnemonic — unusable
description:
Scenario: user runs 'nie wallet restore'. They are prompted for 24 words with rpassword::prompt_password (hidden input, no echo). A typical 24-word mnemonic is ~200 characters. With zero visual feedback, there is no way to detect and correct a typo short of completing the full entry, submitting, and getting a checksum error from bip39. Most users will attempt this multiple times, fail, and assume the feature is broken.

Maintenance consequence: high rate of 'nie wallet restore does not work' issues. The user's mnemonic is correct; the UX is broken. rpassword is appropriate for passphrases (short, known in advance). It is not appropriate for 24 randomly ordered words typed from a paper backup.

Fix: use a visible readline prompt (rustyline is already a dependency) for mnemonic input. The mnemonic is on paper; shoulder-surfing is not the threat model here. Alternatively, accept mnemonic as a file path argument: 'nie wallet restore --mnemonic-file /path/to/backup.txt'.
close_reason: Changed rpassword to rustyline visible input. Decision: mnemonic is on paper, shoulder-surfing not the threat model, usability wins.
